test.name: Extract assertions from a death certificate with a named informant
test.description: Extract assertions from a 1908 Pennsylvania death certificate. The certificate names a specific informant (wife Mary Flynn) whose proximity to the death facts (primary — she was present) differs from her proximity to the birth facts (secondary — she was told, not present). Tests the Informant identification rubric dimension on a non-census record where the informant is explicitly named.

input.user_message:
Extract assertions from this death certificate for Patrick Flynn:

Commonwealth of Pennsylvania — Certificate of Death
Registration District No. 2436 · File No. 89072

Full name: Patrick Flynn
Sex: Male
Color or race: White
Date of death: March 12, 1908
Age: 63 years, 2 months, 10 days
Occupation: Coal miner
Birthplace: Ireland
Father's name: Thomas Flynn
Father's birthplace: Ireland
Mother's maiden name: Mary Brennan
Mother's birthplace: Ireland
Informant: Mary Flynn (wife), 214 Main St, Shenandoah, PA
Cause of death: Miners' asthma (pneumoconiosis)
Duration of last illness: 3 years
Place of death: Shenandoah, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania
Date of burial: March 15, 1908
Place of burial: Annunciation Cemetery, Shenandoah, PA
Undertaker: J.J. Franey, Shenandoah, PA
Attending physician: Dr. William H. Stein

judge_context: The death certificate names Mary Flynn (wife) as the informant — this should appear in the informant field for facts she reported, not 'unknown'

Informant proximity varies by fact: Mary Flynn is PRIMARY for death facts (date, place, cause — she was present/aware) but SECONDARY for birth facts (birthplace, parents' names — she was told these by Patrick or others, not personally present at his birth in Ireland)

The attending physician (Dr. William H. Stein) is the informant for cause of death and duration of illness — he diagnosed it. Mary Flynn may have reported the death date/place but the physician certified the medical cause

Source classification should be DERIVATIVE — a death certificate is created from information provided by the informant and physician, not an original record of the events it describes. The original events (birth in Ireland, death at home) were not recorded by the certificate creator
Death date and death place should be separate atomic assertions — do not bundle them into one assertion. Similarly, age and computed birth year should be separate if both are extracted

Do NOT create a computed birth date assertion (e.g., ~January 2, 1845) — the certificate states age, not birth date. If a birth year is inferred from age, it is indirect evidence and should be labeled as such, but avoid computing exact dates from age/death-date arithmetic

Parents' names and birthplaces reported on a death certificate are INDIRECT evidence — the wife (informant) is reporting what she was told, not what she witnessed. She was not present at Patrick's birth in Ireland and did not personally know his parents' birthplaces. These are secondhand facts relayed through the informant.
